attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of vulval itch and soreness. She reports that the skin appears pale and thickened. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She has not used any over-the-counter treatments. Her cervical screening is up to date. Her mother also has similar symptoms that she has had treated for the previous fifteen years. Her last menses was 4 years prior.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Genital examination reveals white, atrophic patches on the vulva with some architectural distortion. There is no bleeding, ulceration, or obvious masses.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with typical features of vulval lichen sclerosus (LS), a chronic inflammatory condition characterised by white, atrophic patches and architectural changes of the vulva. The condition primarily affects postmenopausal women and can cause significant symptoms including pruritus, dyspareunia, and dysuria. It is thought to be associated with autoimmune conditions, and may be genetic in 15% of cases though the cause is not clear.

Topical clobetasol propionate, a potent topical corticosteroid, is the gold standard first-line treatment. It should be applied once daily for 4-8 weeks, then gradually reduced to a maintenance regimen. This treatment has been shown to improve symptoms in 95% of patients and signs in 75% of patients. Diagnosis of LS may be clinical or by biopsy if there is clinical uncertainty.

Topical oestrogen cream may be helpful for postmenopausal vulval symptoms due to atrophy but is not effective for lichen sclerosus. While both conditions can cause pale, atrophic changes, the architectural distortion described here is more consistent with lichen sclerosus.

Topical antifungal cream would be appropriate for vulvovaginal candidiasis, which typically presents with cottage cheese-like discharge and erythema rather than the white, atrophic changes seen here.

Oral antihistamines may provide symptomatic relief for pruritus but do not address the underlying inflammatory condition and are not recommended as primary therapy. Whilst there is no cause for LS, leaving it untreated and unmonitored from incorrect treatment can increase the risk of progression to vulval cancer.

Topical tacrolimus is sometimes used as a second-line treatment in patients who fail to respond to topical corticosteroids or develop side effects, but it is not recommended as first-line therapy due to limited evidence and theoretical concerns about increased risk of malignancy.

Long-term follow-up is essential as lichen sclerosus is associated with an increased risk of developing vulval squamous cell carcinoma. Patients should be educated about the chronic nature of the condition and the importance of regular moisturiser use and potent topical steroid cream to prevent adhesions and scarring.
learningPoints: Potent topical corticosteroids are first-line treatment for vulval lichen sclerosus.
Regular moisturiser use is an essential part of management to prevent adhesions and scarring.
Long-term follow-up is required due to small risk of malignant transformation.
references: Lichen Sclerosus. Jean Hailes for Women's Health. 2023. Available at: https://www.jeanhailes.org.au/health-a-z/vulva-vagina/vulval-vaginal-conditions/lichen-sclerosus

De Luca DA, Papara C, Vorobyev A, Staiger H, Bieber K, Thaçi D, Ludwig RJ. Lichen sclerosus: The 2023 update. Front Med (Lausanne). 2023. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9978401/
